name: The Particle 了
summary: 了 marks a completed action or a change of situation, doing the work of tense without being one.
explanation:
了 (le) is one of the most useful and most misunderstood particles. It has two main jobs. After a verb it shows the action is complete: 我吃了饭 (wǒ chī le fàn) = I have eaten.

At the end of a sentence it signals a change of state or a new situation: 下雨了 (xià yǔ le) = it has started to rain; 我饿了 (wǒ è le) = I am hungry now (I wasn't before).

Crucially, 了 is not a past tense. Past actions that are habitual or negated do not use it (我没去, I didn't go, has no 了). Think of 了 as 'completed' or 'changed', not 'past'.
pattern: Verb + 了 (completion)  |  ... 了 (change of state, end of sentence)
commonMistakes: Treating 了 as a past tense and adding it to every past sentence. Negated and habitual pasts have no 了.
Using 了 with 没: it is 我没吃, not 我没吃了.
Forgetting that sentence-final 了 means a change, which can be present (我饿了 = I'm hungry now).
Doubling up incorrectly. One 了 per job; do not sprinkle it freely.
Omitting 了 when reporting a completed action, which leaves the sentence sounding unfinished.
tip: Ask two questions: did the action finish, or did something change? If yes to either, you probably need 了. If you are negating with 没, you do not.
